titleStmt.title: Letter from William Upton Tyrrell to Elizabeth Tyrrell 19, December, 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Upton Tyrrell

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from William Upton Tyrrell to his mother.

msIdentifier.repository: OPW-Maynooth University Archive & Research Centre at Castletown

correspAction.placeName: Ballindoolin House, Ballindoolin, Edenderry, Kings Co., Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
19th Decr 1917 My dear Mamy. I was delighted to get your
letter of 12th on the 16th which was
very quick. we did not get any
letters yesterday on the day before
on account of the snow and
moving. We have had a heavy fall
of snow and now very heavy frost. The snow has drifted and is 6 feet
deep in in some places. I think we have toms to rest at least
after all our moving about and are
in a nice little village and quite
comfortable. I wil be able to get letters washed and everything. I told you in my last letter that
I was on ahead of the regiment. Well! After I left their destination was changed but there was no way
of letting me know so I
walked up and down the road
all night waiting for them and
only found out at 10 next morning
that they were in another village 10 miles away - I had a great
tramp through the snow. I had
Morgan Moore with me as he was
on his way back and heard the
batallion were going to the same
place I was in so he came along. I think I got all the parcels you
sent alright except of course the
last trifle which could not
possibly have arrived yet. The plum pudding I could not
keep till Xmas so we just eat it.
It was delicious and
it was awfully good of you to send
them it + particularly the brandy
sauce. I am sure the turkey will
be delicious too. There are only
three of us in the company so we
will have a fine feed. My kit did not come last night
so I had no blankets. I had a nice
big bed and two mattresses so I
just got in between the two and was
as warm as toast. I also had a big
fire so what more could I want. I wrote a few Xmas letters but fear
they will all be late as it was
impossible to get them posted the ar when
we were moving. Things will probably
be more regular now as we will be
here for quite a while I expect. Certainly till next year. No more news and I will write
soon again. I hope you all
got Xmas over successfully. Best love to all Your most affec Son Willie
